faqs.a: Self-compassion is meeting your own struggles with kindness instead of criticism, treating yourself the way you'd treat a good friend who's having a hard time. Researchers describe it as being gentle with yourself, remembering that struggle is part of being human, and noticing painful feelings without getting swept away.

A common worry is that being kind to yourself lets you off the hook. In practice, many people find the opposite, that easing up on harshness makes it easier to face problems rather than hide from them. Self-compassion means facing mistakes without piling on cruelty, not ignoring them.

faqs.q: Is self-compassion the same as self-esteem?
faqs.a: No. Self-esteem often depends on doing well and comparing favorably to others, while self-compassion holds steady even when things go badly. It also isn't self-pity or making excuses. It's a way of relating to yourself that many people find supportive.
